planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, humus-rich soil with some afternoon shade.

directions: Set bulbs a few inches deep in rich woodland soil that stays evenly moist. This is the most cold-hardy spider lily and can persist through mild winters in the ground. Fragrant white flowers with slender segments appear in late summer, later than the coastal species.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant bulbs pointed end up about six inches deep in well-drained soil in autumn. A cold winter chill is required to trigger flowering, so these do poorly in warm-winter regions without pre-chilling. Large scarlet flowers open very early in spring on strong, short stems.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn well before the ground freezes.

directions: Set bulbs about six inches deep in fall in soil that drains freely. Leave the foliage to die back naturally after bloom so the bulb can recharge for next year. Big golden-yellow trumpet flowers appear in early to mid spring and naturalize into large drifts over time.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; excellent for pots and edging.

directions: Plant these small bulbs about four inches deep in well-drained soil or containers in fall. Their compact size makes them ideal for the front of a border or forcing indoors. Cheerful golden flowers, often several per stem, appear very early in spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall; wear gloves as the bulbs can irritate skin.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in fall in rich, well-drained soil. They need a period of winter cold to bloom well and can also be forced in pots indoors. Dense spikes of soft blue, intensely fragrant flowers open in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall, or in early spring where winters are severe.

directions: Plant bulbs about six inches deep in rich, well-drained soil with the roots in cool shade and the tops in sun. Stake tall stems in exposed spots and leave the foliage to feed the bulb after flowering. Upfacing crimson-and-white flowers with a heady perfume open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost; dig and store the tubers before winter in cold areas.

directions: Set tubers about five inches deep in rich soil once nights stay warm, and support the heavy stems with stakes. Cut the huge blooms freely, as harvesting encourages more buds. Dinner-plate flowers in creamy blush tones open from late summer through fall and are prized for arrangements.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes after frost in warm soil; lift for winter storage in cold zones.

directions: Plant rhizomes a few inches deep in rich, moist soil once the ground is warm. Cannas love heat and water and can even grow at a pond's edge. Bold tropical foliage and spikes of red, orange, or yellow flowers carry on from summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in moist, cool soil such as a damp meadow.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about four inches deep in fall in soil that stays reliably moist. They naturalize in damp grass and light woodland where they are left undisturbed. Nodding bells checkered in purple and white, or plain white, appear in mid spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs shallowly in spring against a warm, sheltered wall.

directions: Plant bulbs with their necks at the soil surface in a hot, dry, well-drained spot. They flower best when crowded and left undisturbed for several years. Umbels of frilly pink flowers appear on bare stems in autumn before the leaves.

planting.note: Plant large bulbs in spring with the long neck above the soil surface.

directions: Plant the massive bulbs with their necks well above the soil in deep, rich ground where they can stay put for years. They resent disturbance and bloom better once thoroughly established. Clusters of large pink or white trumpet flowers open on stout stalks in summer and are lightly fragrant.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Nestle bulbs into gritty, well-drained soil with the shoulders exposed and give them full sun. Keep this selection propagated by offsets so its deep velvety-red flowers come true. Blooms rise on solitary stalks in spring and can repeat after dry rest periods through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sharply drained spot or container.

directions: Grow this Mexican species in very gritty mix and give it a warm, dry dormancy. Water sparingly during growth and protect the bulb from winter wet. Slender scarlet flowers with narrow segments open in the warm season, often after summer rains.

planting.note: Set bulbs out in spring once soil is warm; excellent in pots.

directions: Pot this uncommon species in a lean, free-draining mix and keep it dry when dormant. The gray-blue foliage sets off the bright red spring flowers. Give a warm, bright position and avoid soggy soil that can rot the bulb.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring for summer bloom; naturalizes well in warm zones.

directions: Set bulbs about two inches deep in moist, well-drained soil and plant in clusters for the best show. Flushes of large soft-pink, crocus-shaped blooms appear a few days after summer rains or a deep watering. Let clumps stay undisturbed and they will slowly seed and spread.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerant of poor soils and easy to naturalize.

directions: Tuck small bulbs an inch or two deep into gritty soil and let them dry between waterings. Coppery-yellow flowers stained bronze on the outside open after late-summer rain. This is one of the easiest rain lilies and self-sows freely where happy.

planting.note: Plant in spring; among the most cold-hardy of the rain lilies.

directions: Plant bulbs a couple inches deep in lean, sharply drained soil and full sun. Golden flowers backed with coppery-red open after summer storms and multiply readily from seed. This hardy Gulf Coast form takes brief freezes and naturalizes in lawns and meadows.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty soil; good in troughs and containers.

directions: Grow this Argentine species in a sharply drained mix and keep it on the dry side. Soft-yellow to peach flowers appear on short stems after warm-season rains. Divide crowded clumps in spring to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the larger-flowered rain lilies.

directions: Set bulbs about two inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Large pale-pink flowers with a deep maroon throat rise after summer rains, one per stem. Plant in generous drifts and let seed scatter to build a colony over the years.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed or pot.

directions: Plant bulbs a couple inches deep among the fine, grassy foliage this species is named for. Pink to rose flowers open after warm rains on slim stalks. Keep soil moist during growth and drier during dormancy for reliable repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a clear-yellow Mexican rain lily.

directions: Set bulbs an inch or two deep in gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun. Uniform lemon-yellow flowers open a few days after summer rain. Give a warm, dry rest in winter and avoid waterlogged conditions.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a dainty small-flowered species for troughs.

directions: Grow this petite species in a lean mix with excellent drainage. Small pink flowers sit just above thread-fine, rush-like leaves after warm rains. Ideal for pots and rock gardens where its delicate scale can be appreciated.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharply drained soil and full sun.

directions: Plant bulbs a couple inches deep in gritty soil and water after warm-season storms. Rich rose-purple flowers open on short stems following rain. Keep the bulbs dry during winter dormancy to prevent rot.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring once the soil has warmed and hard frost has passed.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the soil surface and water regularly through the first season while roots establish. Give it room, as clumps widen steadily each year and the coppery-silver plumes rise well above the foliage in late summer. Leave the dried stalks standing for winter interest and cut the whole clump to a few inches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the soil warms so roots settle before summer heat.

directions: Set the crown at soil level and keep evenly moist while the fine, thread-like leaves fill out into a graceful fountain. Copper plumes fade to silver arrive in early autumn and hold through winter. Shear the clump to the ground in late winter before new shoots emerge.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Give it full sun so the yellow cross-bands stay bold and the stiff blades hold upright like quills. Plant the crown level and water through the first summer to establish a dense clump. Bronze plumes rise in autumn above the foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for a full clump by late summer.

directions: Position where backlight can catch the fine white-edged blades that give the clump a shimmering, silvery halo. Keep evenly watered the first year, then it tolerates occasional dry spells. Reddish plumes appear late in the season and age to silver.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring as new growth begins.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade where the creamy-white lengthwise stripes brighten the border. The soft foliage may need staking in wind or rich soil. Coppery plumes emerge in fall and fade to a pale tan.

planting.note: Set out in spring; this compact selection fills in quickly.

directions: Choose this smaller cultivar for tighter spaces where full-size maiden grasses would overwhelm. Plant the crown at soil level and water to establish. Pink-tinged plumes rise in late summer, ripening to silver-white and standing well above the fine gray-green blades.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for reliable late-summer bloom.

directions: A vigorous early bloomer prized for its large burgundy-red plumes that open in late summer and mature to fluffy silver. Plant in full sun and water through establishment. Foliage takes on orange and red tints in autumn before fading to tan.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this dwarf form stays neat and upright.

directions: A compact, fine-textured selection well suited to smaller gardens and containers. Set the crown level and keep watered the first season. Coppery plumes rise above the narrow foliage in early fall and turn silvery with age.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring for tall late-summer plumes.

directions: One of the earliest and most free-flowering maiden grasses, sending up silvery-pink plumes on tall stems by late summer. Give it full sun and space to reach its considerable height. Leave the seed heads for winter and cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this dwarf zebra type is slow to spread.

directions: A compact zebra-banded selection with bright yellow bars packed densely along short, stout blades. Plant in full sun for the boldest banding and water to establish. Burgundy plumes appear late in the season on this smaller-scale clump.

planting.note: Set out in spring; a tidy dwarf that suits small beds.

directions: A dwarf zebra grass with horizontal gold banding on upright, non-flopping blades. Plant the crown level in full sun and water through the first summer. Reddish-purple plumes open in early fall above the compact mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring for the most dramatic first-fall color.

directions: Grown chiefly for the fiery orange-red foliage that ignites in autumn, earlier and more cold-hardy than most maiden grasses. Plant in full sun for the strongest color and water to establish. Silvery plumes rise above the blades in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; slightly less cold-hardy than most.

directions: A bold, wide-bladed maiden grass with a broad creamy-white center stripe edged in green. Plant in full sun and give it space for the substantial clump. Wine-red plumes open in fall and fade to silver above the striking foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give this large grass ample room.

directions: A large, dramatic grass with broad blades edged in bright white, making a strong specimen or screen. Plant in full sun and water well while establishing. Reddish plumes appear in autumn above foliage that can reach eight feet.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring for early, abundant plumes.

directions: An early-flowering selection with fine green foliage and large silvery-white plumes held above the clump. Plant in full sun and keep watered the first year. Blades turn coppery-orange in fall, complementing the pale seed heads.

planting.note: Plant in spring for reddish plumes the same season.

directions: Named for the reddish plumes that open in late summer and shift to silver as they mature. Plant the crown level in full sun and water to establish. The green foliage develops orange and red autumn tones.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for an early, long bloom season.

directions: An early and generous bloomer whose arching plumes emerge reddish and cascade like a fountain. Plant in full sun and water while establishing. Its long flowering season makes it a favorite for smaller borders.

planting.note: Set out in spring for rich red plumes by late summer.

directions: A compact selection with narrow blades and deep ruby-red plumes that catch the light beautifully. Plant in full sun and water to establish. The foliage reddens in fall as the plumes fade to silvery-white.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a cold-hardy, early-flowering selection.

directions: A hardy, free-flowering grass bred for reliable performance in colder gardens. Plant in full sun and water through the first season. Reddish-silver plumes rise well above the fine foliage in late summer and persist into winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; an upright, early bloomer.

directions: A narrow, upright selection that flowers early and holds its form without staking. Plant the crown level in full sun and water to establish. Coppery-red plumes appear in late summer and the foliage turns orange-red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring for large pink-toned plumes by late summer.

directions: Grown for its large, arching plumes that open a soft pink before fading to silver. Plant in full sun and water while roots establish. The nodding flower heads sway gracefully and remain attractive into the cooler months.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring for arching late-summer plumes.

directions: A graceful selection with fine foliage and reddish plumes that arch outward and lighten to silver. Plant in full sun and water to establish the clump. Leave the seed heads standing for winter texture and cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring; this sterile hybrid does not set viable seed.

directions: An enormous, fast-growing clumping grass often used as a summer screen or living wall. Plant in full sun with room to reach ten feet or more, and water generously the first year. Feathery plumes may appear in warm autumns; cut the tall canes to the ground in late winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this sterile hybrid sets no seed.

directions: A strongly vertical grass whose feathery flower stalks shoot up early in the season and hold upright all summer. Plant in full sun to light shade and it tolerates heavier, moist soils. The plumes emerge pinkish, ripen to golden-tan, and stand through winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; a slow-spreading shade grass.

directions: A softly cascading grass with bright gold blades finely striped in green, glowing in shady borders and containers. Plant in partial shade with rich, moisture-retentive soil, as too much sun scorches the leaves. The foliage takes on pink and russet tints in autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; spreads slowly to form a groundcover.

directions: A tough, semi-evergreen sedge with dark green blades crisply edged in white that brighten shady spots. Plant in partial shade to full shade in moist soil and it slowly runs to knit together as groundcover. Trim tattered foliage in early spring to freshen the clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost, giving this tall selection room.

directions: Give this towering blue-green grass full sun and space at the back of a border. Set the crown level with the soil and firm it in; it stays surprisingly upright for its height. Loose airy panicles float well above the foliage from late summer into fall, and the plant fades to warm gold before winter.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Plant in full sun with the crown at grade; its unusually broad powder-blue blades give a bolder texture than most switchgrasses. Large purple-tinted flower panicles open in late summer and soften to pinkish tan. Leave the clump standing through winter and cut it back before spring growth.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Choose a lean, sunny, well-drained spot to keep this deep-blue selection from flopping, since rich or shaded conditions make it lean. Set the crown at soil level and water until established. Airy panicles rise in late summer above the arching blue foliage; cut the plant down in early spring.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring once soil warms.

directions: Plant this classic German selection in full sun with the crown level. Green summer blades take on red tones by late summer and turn wine-red in autumn. Fine reddish flower heads appear in late summer; leave the foliage for winter and shear it in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost has passed.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in a sunny, well-drained bed. The narrow blades flush red-brown in late summer and deepen through fall. Airy panicles float above the clump in late summer; cut the standing stems back to a few inches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant in full sun with the crown at grade for the sturdiest habit. Green foliage develops burgundy tips as summer ends and reddens in fall. Reddish-purple flower panicles open in late summer; leave the plant standing over winter and trim in spring.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun with the crown level with the soil. Blue-green blades turn wine-red early in summer and hold rich color into fall. Airy pink-tinted panicles appear in late summer; cut the clump back in early spring before new shoots emerge.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once the soil warms.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in full sun and average, well-drained ground. This selection has a somewhat arching form and colors orange-red in autumn. Fine flower panicles rise in late summer; leave the foliage for winter texture and shear it in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant in full sun with the crown at grade. The foliage flushes red in late summer and the plant carries abundant reddish-pink flower heads. Leave the clump standing through winter for structure and cut it back to a few inches in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring, allowing room for a tall clump.

directions: Give this tall, upright blue-green grass full sun near the back of a planting. Set the crown at soil level and water until rooted; it holds its vertical form well. Large cloud-like panicles open in late summer well above the foliage, and the plant turns golden in fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost for a tidy small clump.

directions: Plant this dwarf switchgrass in full sun with the crown at grade; its small footprint suits containers and small beds. Blue-green blades turn wine-red early, by midsummer, and hold color into fall. Reddish flower panicles top the compact clump; cut it back in early spring.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant in full sun with the crown level with the soil. Blue-green upright foliage is topped in late summer by distinctive dark purple seed heads that read almost black at a distance. Leave the panicles standing for winter interest and cut the plant back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in full sun for the best color and form. Blue-green blades take on rosy tints as the season cools and the plant carries airy rose-pink flower panicles in late summer. Leave the clump for winter and cut it down in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring, allowing space for a very tall clump.

directions: Give this exceptionally tall, blue-foliaged switchgrass full sun and room at the back of a border. Set the crown at grade and water until established; it stays upright and can top eight feet in bloom. Flower panicles open in late summer high above the foliage, aging to warm tan for winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost for a compact clump.

directions: Plant this small, richly colored selection in full sun with the crown at grade. Foliage turns red early, from the base upward, reaching deep burgundy by late summer. Fine flower panicles top the compact clump; leave it for winter and shear back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once the soil warms.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in full sun. This selection has extra-fine, tousled green foliage that gives a soft billowing texture and turns gold in fall. Delicate flower panicles rise above the clump in late summer; cut the plant back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant in full sun with the crown level with the soil. Green blades develop red tips in summer and turn deep burgundy-red as nights cool. Airy reddish panicles appear in late summer; leave the foliage for winter and trim it in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for a low, tidy clump.

directions: Plant this compact, salt-tolerant selection in full sun with the crown at grade; it suits small beds and coastal gardens. Green blades form a neat mound topped by airy panicles in late summer. Leave the clump standing over winter and cut it back in early spring.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring; excellent for sandy, salt-exposed sites.

directions: Barely cover seed in sandy soil, or set plants in spring; this dune native thrives in heat, drought, and salt spray. Give it full sun and sharp drainage. Blue-green blades form loose clumps topped by narrow flower panicles in late summer that hold as tan seed heads into winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; ideal for sandy, seaside conditions.

directions: Plant this powder-blue selection in full sun with the crown at grade; it excels in sandy, salty, and dry sites. Set the roots firmly and water until established. Arching blue blades are topped by airy panicles in late summer that fade to tan and persist through winter.

planting.note: Start seed indoors a few weeks before the last frost, or sow outside once warm.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep it warm and moist; it needs light to germinate. Grow this tender annual in full sun and move it out after frost. Sparkling silvery flower panicles explode above the foliage in summer and are prized fresh or dried for arrangements.

planting.note: Sow directly after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Sow seed shallowly into warm soil in full sun; this native annual grass germinates quickly and self-sows readily. Give it lean ground and steady warmth. Enormous lacy flower panicles form a hazy cloud by late summer, then break away like tumbleweeds as the plant dries.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring once soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seed shallowly or set plants in spring in full sun and well-drained soil; this Southwest native forms swollen bulb-like bases that help it endure drought. Give it lean ground and infrequent deep watering. Slender flower panicles rise above the clump in late summer and dry to warm tan.

planting.note: Set plants out once the soil has warmed after the last spring frost.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep the surface warm and evenly moist until sprouts appear. Give each clump room to fountain outward, and water regularly through the first season while roots establish. Bottlebrush plumes rise above the arching foliage from mid to late summer and hold their tan color into winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as the soil warms so they root before summer heat.

directions: Set divisions at the same depth they grew before and firm the soil around the crown. This compact selection blooms earlier and shorter than the species, so it suits smaller beds and edging. Cut the clump back to a few inches in late winter before new growth pushes up.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; give it room as it can self-sow freely.

directions: Plant divisions in warm soil and keep them watered while they settle in. Prized for its dark, near-black bottlebrush plumes that emerge in early autumn above glossy foliage. Deadhead spent plumes if you want to limit its enthusiastic self-seeding.

planting.note: Set out divisions after the last frost as soil warms.

directions: Plant at the original depth and water regularly the first year. This selection is valued for outstanding fall color, with foliage turning gold and orange as the season cools. Tan plumes appear in late summer above the arching leaves.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Give this larger cultivar plenty of space and firm the soil around each division. It produces exceptionally large smoky-purple plumes that hold their color well into fall. Leave the seed heads standing for winter interest and cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as soil warms.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained soil and water them in. A dwarf selection sitting between the tiniest and mid-size fountain grasses, good for the front of a border. Foxtail plumes rise just above the mound in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Allow room for this tall, upright cultivar and water regularly while it establishes. It sends rosy foxtail plumes high above the foliage from midsummer, making a strong vertical accent. Cut the clump down in late winter before fresh blades emerge.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant divisions in a sunny spot with good drainage and water in well. This dwarf grass greens up in spring then flushes deep red through summer, intensifying to burgundy in fall. Small tan plumes accent the colorful foliage in late season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as the soil warms.

directions: Set divisions at their original depth and keep evenly moist through establishment. This selection carries dark, dusky plumes over deep green foliage from late summer. Leave the heads for winter texture and trim back before spring growth.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a well-drained, sunny spot.

directions: Plant divisions in lean soil with excellent drainage and avoid overwatering. Long rosy-purple plumes wave above the foliage from early summer and keep coming for months. Enjoy its long bloom window and cut the clump back in late winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost; give this large grass plenty of room.

directions: Space this vigorous grass generously and plant in well-drained soil. It forms a large fountain topped by long creamy-white plumes that arch outward in summer. Use it as a specimen or screen where its size can be appreciated.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set divisions into gritty, well-drained soil and water sparingly once established. This robust selection carries abundant soft pink plumes over a long summer season. Leave it standing into autumn for continued interest.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; treat as an annual outside frost-free zones.

directions: Start seed warm and barely covered, then move seedlings out once nights stay mild. This green-leaved species blooms quickly with fluffy pink-tinged plumes over a long warm season. Note that it can be invasive in mild climates, so deadhead to prevent unwanted seeding.

planting.note: Set out after frost; grown as an annual where winters freeze.

directions: Plant from divisions or pots into warm soil in full sun for the best leaf color. This variegated sport shows brilliant stripes of burgundy, hot pink, and green that glow when backlit. Feathery plumes rise above the colorful foliage through summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant only after frost danger passes; annual in cold regions.

directions: Grow this cultivar in full sun and warm soil to bring out its pink-and-cream variegation. It offers softer, cherry-toned striping than its parent and pairs well in mixed containers. Airy plumes appear over the foliage as the season warms.

planting.note: Set out after frost; treat as an annual where not hardy.

directions: Plant in full sun and warm soil for the crispest white-and-green variegation. This upright selection makes a bright vertical accent in beds and large pots. Pinkish plumes rise above the striped blades through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant after frost; grown as an annual in cold-winter areas.

directions: Set divisions or plants into warm, sunny ground for the deepest foliage color. A compact purple-leaved selection that fits smaller containers and tighter spaces than standard purple fountain grass. Rosy plumes accent the burgundy blades through summer.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings after frost once soil is warm.

directions: Sow seed shallowly indoors a few weeks before the last frost and grow warm in strong light. Full sun deepens the striking near-black foliage on this upright annual millet. Cattail-like plumes form at the tips in summer and dry to feed seed-eating birds.

planting.note: Set out after frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Start seed indoors barely covered and keep warm and bright until transplanting. Foliage emerges lime green and shifts to deep burgundy in full sun as plants mature. Thick plumes rise above the leaves in late summer for a bold vertical accent.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow shallowly indoors and grow warm before moving seedlings out after frost. This compact selection holds deep purple foliage on a sturdier, shorter frame than taller millets. Dark plumes top the plants in summer and hold well for cutting.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when nights stay warm.

directions: Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant once frost has passed. Foliage takes on warm coppery-bronze tones in strong sun, offering a different palette than the purple millets. Plumes appear at the stem tips in late summer above the arching leaves.

planting.note: Set out after frost; grown as an annual outside mild zones.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warmth and transplant after the last frost. This grass is grown for its soft, fluffy cream-white plumes that resemble feathery caterpillars. Cut some plumes for fresh or dried arrangements, and deadhead to curb self-sowing in mild areas.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained soil and water sparingly once rooted. This upright hybrid produces slender, soft creamy plumes over a long summer season. Its narrow habit makes it a graceful accent in borders and large containers.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost in a warm, sunny bed.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained soil in full sun and water moderately to establish. Its distinctive plumes open as short, red-tinged buttons before elongating, giving a unique two-stage look. Enjoy the flowers from midsummer into fall and cut back in late winter.

planting.note: Plant after frost; can spread by rhizomes so site it with care.

directions: Start seed warm or plant divisions after frost, allowing generous room. This tall grass sends up narrow, poker-like cream plumes on wiry stems well above the foliage. Because it can spread aggressively, consider containing it or removing spent heads.

planting.note: Set out after frost; grown as an annual in cold regions.

directions: Plant from divisions or pots into warm soil in full sun and give it plenty of room. This bold napier grass grows quickly to a towering size with wide burgundy-black leaves. It rarely blooms in cool summers but earns its place as a dramatic foliage centerpiece.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as the soil warms.

directions: Set divisions at their original depth and water through establishment. This selection is noted for yellow-toned stems and strong golden autumn color. Bottlebrush plumes arch above the foliage from late summer and persist into winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; may self-sow in favorable spots.

directions: Plant divisions in warm soil and keep watered while they take hold. This botanical variety blooms early with dark, blackish plumes over broad green foliage. Deadhead if you wish to limit its tendency to seed around the garden.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm soil.

directions: Start seed indoors barely covered and grow warm and bright before transplanting. This selection keeps bright chartreuse-green foliage that contrasts sharply with its dark reddish plumes. Give it full sun for the best leaf color and plume production through the season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Set divisions into lean, well-drained soil and water sparingly once established. This selection carries large soft-pink plumes that arch over the foliage through summer. Its drought tolerance suits sunny, low-water borders and gravel gardens.

planting.note: Set out potted divisions once nights stay above freezing in spring.

directions: Plant divisions at the depth they grew in the pot and firm the soil around the crown. Selected for strong blue foliage, it colors best in lean soil and full sun, so skip the fertilizer. Watch the upright blue clumps flush purple and red by autumn above shining seed heads.

planting.note: Transplant container-grown clumps in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Space plants for good airflow and set the crown level with the surrounding grade. Prized for stiff, non-flopping stems, it holds an upright vase shape even after summer storms. Blue-green columns shift to orange, red, and purple tones as the season closes.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring; a Minnesota selection tough in cold zones.

directions: Set divisions into well-drained ground and water sparingly once roots take hold. Bred for tall, upright habit and steely blue summer color, it resists lodging better than seed strains. Fall brings burgundy and mahogany tints that linger into winter.

planting.note: Sow the seed strain in warm spring soil for a blue-toned drift.

directions: Broadcast seed onto firmed soil and rake in lightly, keeping it barely covered. This seed-grown strain shows more blue foliage than the wild type but varies plant to plant. Expect coppery fall color and fluffy silver seed heads by late summer.

planting.note: Plant compact divisions in spring for a tidy mid-border grass.

directions: Set divisions level with the soil and give them a sunny, well-drained spot. This compact selection stays denser and shorter than the species, holding its form neatly. Blue-green foliage turns a mix of pink, orange, and burgundy in cool weather.

planting.note: Transplant in spring; grown for its brilliant autumn color.

directions: Plant into lean, well-drained soil and avoid rich beds that cause flopping. Selected for intense fall color, it lights up in shades of red and orange as temperatures drop. Leave the clumps standing through winter for structure and wildlife cover.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring for a shorter, blue-hued clump.

directions: Firm the soil around each division and water only until established. This compact, blue-leaved selection keeps a rounded, upright form in tight spaces. Cool nights bring rosy purple and copper tones to the foliage.

planting.note: Plant potted divisions in spring for shimmering silvery-purple color.

directions: Set the crown at grade in a sunny, sharply drained bed. Its foliage shows a metallic silver-blue overlaid with purple that deepens as summer advances. By fall the whole clump glows in rose, red, and violet hues.

planting.note: Transplant in spring; upright habit with early fall color change.

directions: Plant divisions in lean soil and full sun for the strongest color and posture. Narrow, strictly upright columns start blue-green and shift early to deep red and purple. The tight vertical form makes a clean accent in mass plantings.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for a multicolored, shifting foliage display.

directions: Set into well-drained soil and grow hard without extra feeding. The foliage shifts through blue, pink, and burgundy blends as the season progresses. Cool fall weather intensifies the pink and red tones across the clump.

planting.note: Transplant in spring; a robust Missouri-origin selection.

directions: Plant divisions at their original depth and firm the soil around them. This vigorous selection forms broad, upright clumps with good drought endurance. Green-blue summer color gives way to warm copper and red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant compact divisions in spring for edging or containers.

directions: Set this dwarf selection into a sunny, well-drained spot and keep it lean. Its short, dense clumps suit the front of a border or a mixed container. Blue-green blades warm to orange and red as the growing season winds down.

planting.note: Sow in warm sandy soil; a coastal species tolerant of salt and dunes.

directions: Press seed into loose sandy soil where it can spread and stabilize the ground. This coastal relative of little bluestem handles salt spray, wind, and dune conditions. Low, spreading clumps turn tawny bronze with silvery seed heads in fall.

planting.note: Sow in warm soil; a heat-loving species best in mild-winter regions.

directions: Sow into warm, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist until the seedlings root in. This warm-region species forms tufted clumps topped by reddish flowering stems. Foliage and seed heads take on rich crimson and bronze tones in fall.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm, sandy ground in late spring.

directions: Scatter seed onto loose sandy soil and rake in shallowly, since this grass is built for dry sandhill sites. Its spreading rhizomes knit loose ground together, so give it room and expect it to fill in over a few seasons. The waxy blue-green blades take on bronze tones and send up feathery seed stalks by early autumn.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring to keep the dark color true.

directions: Plant a division or container plant at the same depth it grew and water it in while roots take hold. Grow in full sun and lean soil for the strongest color, as shade and rich ground wash it out and cause flopping. The near-black blades deepen through summer and the clump is topped by dark reddish seed heads in fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for best establishment before summer heat.

directions: Set the plant in full sun with good air movement and firm the soil around the crown before watering. It needs little care once rooted and stands upright without staking in most sites. Green summer blades are tipped burgundy, then the whole plant blazes wine-red after the first cool nights of autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, open spot.

directions: Place a division at its original depth in full sun and water regularly only until it is established. This selection stays narrow and vertical, making it a good screen or accent in tight borders. The upright blades take on deep red and burgundy tones as the season cools, holding through fall.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring for a strong first-year clump.

directions: Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, tamping the roots in firmly before the first watering. Grown hard it stays stiffly upright and needs no support even in exposed spots. Blue-green summer foliage shifts to purple-red in autumn beneath its forked seed heads.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; give this tall selection room.

directions: Give this large grass full sun and space, setting divisions at their prior depth and watering to settle the roots. It is drought-tolerant once established and rarely needs feeding. The tall blue-green stems flush burgundy and red in fall and sway freely in the wind, as the name suggests.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Set the plant in full sun and firm the soil around the crown, watering until new growth appears. Lean soil and bright light keep it upright and bring out the richest tones. Smoky blue-gray blades pick up wine-red tips through summer and glow deep red as autumn arrives.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring for the bluest first-season color.

directions: Plant in full sun and dry, well-drained soil to bring out the powdery silver-blue foliage this selection is known for. It is heat- and drought-tolerant once rooted and stands tall without staking. The blue blades take on lavender and burgundy shades in fall above forked seed heads.

planting.note: Set out container plants or divisions in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before and firm the soil around the roots. Prized for its strongly upright, silvery blue-green summer foliage, this selection stays tidy in full sun and lean soil; cut the old growth back to a few inches in late winter. Narrow bronze-gold seed heads appear atop the stiff stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant nursery stock or divisions in spring after the ground warms.

directions: Space clumps far enough apart to show off the vertical form and set them at their original soil depth. The steely blue-gray blades take on orange and burgundy tints in autumn, so site it where the fall color reads against a darker backdrop. Golden-tan plumes rise in late summer and hold well into winter if left standing.

planting.note: Install container plants in spring once the soil is workable and warming.

directions: Give this tall, robust selection room and full sun so the strong stems do not flop. It needs little supplemental water once rooted and thrives in average to lean soil. Heavy, deep-bronze flower heads open in late summer above the green-blue foliage, which colors to gold and russet before winter.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring after the last frost as soil warms.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained spot at the depth the clump was already growing. This selection is grown for its warm golden fall foliage and upright habit, and it asks for little once established. Amber flower plumes appear in late summer and catch low autumn light well.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm spring soil or transplant plugs after frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the surface moist while it germinates in warm weather. This southeastern native has a more open, arching habit than common Indian grass and takes light shade and average moisture. Its delicate, nodding golden-bronze flower panicles sway on wiry stems from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Sow into warm spring soil or set out plugs after the last frost.

directions: Press the seed lightly into warm, sandy soil and keep it damp until it sprouts. Native to Gulf Coast pinelands, this grass shrugs off heat, drought, and lean sandy ground once rooted. Its distinctive one-sided flower spike, with golden florets angled to a single side of the stalk, opens in early fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a spot with afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Set divisions level with the surrounding soil and water them in well. This white-margined selection keeps its variegation best with some relief from intense afternoon sun in warm regions. Slender plumes appear in early summer; shear the whole clump to a few inches in late winter before new growth begins.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and average, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant crown divisions at their original depth and keep them watered through the first season. This selection carries a bright white stripe down the center of each blade rather than along the margins. Airy vertical plumes rise in early summer and dry to tan; cut old growth back hard in late winter.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in full sun for the strongest gold coloring.

directions: Plant divisions level with the soil surface and water them in thoroughly. The golden-centered blades hold their color best in full sun and cooler summers. Narrow upright plumes emerge in early summer; remove the previous year's foliage in late winter before growth resumes.

planting.note: Plant divisions or sow seed in spring in a spot with good drainage.

directions: Sow seed shallowly or set out divisions in spring, and give this California native excellent drainage. It forms a low, arching mound of blue-green foliage and prefers dry summers, so avoid overwatering established plants. Feathery flower spikes tinged purple and buff arch out over the leaves from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or sow in spring; tolerates coastal shade and moist ground.

directions: Barely cover seed or plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich soil. This tough West Coast native handles salt spray, shade, and seasonally wet ground better than most reed grasses. Narrow, upright flowering stems appear in summer and dry to a warm tan, persisting into the cool season.

planting.note: Sow seed on moist soil in spring; ideal for damp meadows and rain gardens.

directions: Surface-sow seed on consistently moist ground in spring, as it needs light to germinate. This cold-hardy North American native spreads by rhizomes and is best suited to wet meadows, pond edges, and restoration plantings. Loose, purplish-tan flower panicles open in early to midsummer and fade to soft beige.

planting.note: Sow in spring, but site with care as this species spreads aggressively by rhizomes.

directions: Surface-sow seed in spring and be aware that this vigorous Eurasian grass runs by rhizomes and can colonize disturbed ground. Plant it only where its spread can be contained or where erosion control is needed. Feathery, purplish flower plumes rise in summer and mature to a dull straw color.

planting.note: Sow seed or plant divisions in spring; tolerates light woodland shade.

directions: Barely cover seed or plant divisions in spring in a spot with dappled shade and moist, well-drained soil. This clump-forming woodland species stays more compact than the garden hybrids and reads well in shadier borders. Airy, arching flower panicles open in summer and dry to warm tan.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring so the clump settles before summer heat.

directions: Set divisions with the crown level with the soil and space them a hand's width apart for a tidy row of blue globes. This selection holds its powder-blue color longer than the species; divide congested clumps every two or three years to keep the center from dying out. Comb out dead blades in spring and trim off the wheat-colored flower stems after they fade if you prefer a neat mound.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring into gritty, fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant divisions crown-high and firm the soil gently around each blue tuft. Prized for an intense, almost silver-blue cast that resists fading in heat, it wants full sun and near-drought conditions once rooted. Rake out spent blades each spring and lift and split the clumps every few years to renew their vigor and color.

planting.note: Plant divisions in cool spring weather for even establishment.

directions: Set the crown at soil level and space evenly for a run of uniform blue cushions. The German name means Blue Fox, a nod to its fine, bright-blue needle foliage that reads well in mass plantings and rock gardens. Give it sun and lean drainage, shear low in early spring, and remove tan seed heads for a cleaner look.

planting.note: Install divisions in spring into full sun and gritty soil.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at grade and keep them lightly watered until new roots grip. This selection, whose name translates to Blue Glow, carries a strong silvery-blue tone that stays vivid in sun. Trim the mound back in early spring and divide every couple of years to prevent the tuft from opening up in the middle.

planting.note: Plant nursery divisions in spring; this patented cultivar is not grown from saved seed.

directions: Set divisions crown-high in fast-draining soil and water sparingly once established. Selected for an unusually deep blue color that holds well through summer heat and humidity, it stays compact and tidy with little care. Rake out old blades in spring; it flowers modestly and is grown chiefly for its year-round foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into sun and gritty soil.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and firm gently around each icy-blue tuft. This selection is noted for a pale, frosty silver-blue color that stays bright in strong sun. Provide excellent drainage, shear low in early spring, and divide crowded clumps to keep the mounds dense.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; give a little afternoon shade in hot climates to protect the foliage.

directions: Set divisions crown-high and space them for a bright ribbon of chartreuse mounds. Unlike its blue kin, this sport shows soft yellow-green to golden foliage that glows best in cooler sun and can scorch in intense afternoon heat. Trim back in spring and divide periodically to refresh color and form.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: Set the crown at grade and water lightly until rooted, then keep the site on the dry side. This selection is grown for a strong, saturated blue that holds through the season in full sun. Comb out dead blades in early spring and lift and divide every few years to keep the tufts tight.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring into lean, gritty soil.

directions: Plant divisions crown-high and space them closely for a cluster of spiky blue balls. The German name means Sea Urchin, describing the fine, radiating needle-blue leaves that form neat rounded cushions. Give it full sun and dry, well-drained soil, shear in early spring, and divide as clumps age.

planting.note: Sow in cool spring soil or set divisions once the ground is workable.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it moist until the fine blades appear in about two to three weeks. This species forms a larger, softer mound than common blue fescue, with blue-green foliage and amethyst-tinged flower stems in early summer. Give it full sun and good drainage, and shear the clump in spring to freshen the growth.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring so plumes develop the first full season.

directions: Set divisions with the crown at soil level and give each room to form a full mound. This selection is grown for showy amethyst-pink flower stalks that rise on reddish stems above blue-green foliage in early summer. Provide full sun and sharp drainage, and cut back old growth in early spring before new blades push.

planting.note: Sow in spring or fall; this western native reseeds readily in lean ground.

directions: Scatter seed on a firmed bed and rake it in lightly, keeping the surface moist until the fine blue-green blades appear. A tough, drought-tolerant native of western North America, it forms dense bunches and is valued for meadow and restoration plantings. Let the airy flower stalks ripen to tan in summer, or shear the clump for a tidier mound.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Set divisions crown-high and space them to allow full, arching mounds. This selection is larger and more graceful than common blue fescue, with soft silvery-blue foliage that spills into a fountain shape. Give it sun and lean drainage, comb out old blades in spring, and divide crowded clumps to keep the color strong.

planting.note: Sow in fall or cool spring; this native tolerates part shade under oaks.

directions: Press seed into a firmed bed and keep it lightly moist until the broad blue-green blades emerge. A large, clumping California native, it thrives in dappled shade beneath oaks and puts up tall, airy flower panicles in late spring. Water occasionally the first summer, then let it go largely dry, and cut back tattered clumps in late winter.

planting.note: Sow in spring or set divisions; it makes a low, mossy cushion.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it moist until the stiff green needles appear. Also sold as spiky or hedgehog fescue, it forms a very low, dense emerald cushion that looks like a tuft of moss and stays bright green rather than blue. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, and simply rake out any winter-burned tips in spring.

planting.note: Sow in spring; this Moroccan species handles more heat and humidity than blue fescue.

directions: Sow into a firmed bed and keep evenly moist until the arching gray-green blades appear. A large, fountain-shaped fescue from the Atlas Mountains, it tolerates heat and humidity better than most and sends up tall, oat-like flower stems in early summer. Give it full sun and room to spread, and cut the clump back hard in late winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into gritty, fast-draining soil.

directions: Set divisions crown-high and space them close for a run of tiny silver cushions. Sometimes sold as Silver Sea, this dwarf selection has extremely fine, silvery-blue hair-like foliage and stays smaller than common blue fescue. Give it full sun and lean, dry soil, and comb out old blades in early spring to keep the tuft crisp.

planting.note: Sow in spring or set divisions into very sharply drained soil.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it moist until the stiff blue needles appear. This Turkish species forms a compact cushion of rigid, sharply pointed ice-blue leaves that genuinely feel prickly, giving it its common name. Give it full sun and gritty, dry soil, avoid winter wet, and tidy the cushion by hand in spring rather than shearing.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring to root in before summer heat.

directions: Propagate this selection by division rather than seed so the intense blue color and improved rust resistance stay true. Plant into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and keep the crown from sitting in moisture over winter. Comb out spent foliage in early spring, and expect wiry flower stems to rise above the crisp blue tuft in early summer.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings out in spring after the last hard frost has passed.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on a lean, calcareous, free-draining mix and keep it lightly moist until sprouts appear. This tufted meadow species prefers full sun and dry, alkaline ground and dislikes rich or soggy soil. Trim old growth in early spring; airy flowering panicles emerge above the blue-green tuft in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Move young plants to the garden in spring once frost danger fades.

directions: Start seed on the surface of a well-drained tray and cover only lightly, as this grass sprouts best with some light. Give it full sun and moderately fertile, well-drained soil; the soft, hairy leaf bases give the plant its common name. Cut back tired foliage in late winter, and watch for loose, shimmering flower heads over the green clump in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Firm the root ball in at grade and water well to settle the soil. The arching leaves carry crisp cream-white edges that brighten shaded borders and containers. Trim out any browned blades in early spring before new growth appears.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in fertile, evenly moist soil in part shade.

directions: Plant so the crown sits at grade and keep the roots cool with a light mulch. The foliage emerges lime green and matures to solid glowing gold, brightest in a bit of shade. Comb out tired leaves in early spring to showcase the new flush.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant level with the surrounding soil and water in thoroughly. The long green blades carry clean white stripes and cascade into a full mound larger than most oshimensis types. Refresh the clump by removing weathered leaves before spring growth.

planting.note: Plant after frost danger passes in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this New Zealand sedge in sharply drained soil and give it full sun for the best color. The hair-fine reddish-bronze blades weep to the ground in a shaggy, informal mound. Leave the clump uncut and simply rake out loose dead strands with your fingers in spring.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in sun or light shade in soil that drains freely to avoid winter rot. The extremely fine, arching blades form a soft weeping mound in green or bronze forms. Comb out loose old strands by hand rather than shearing, which spoils the shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and sharply drained soil.

directions: Grow this sedge in full sun, which draws out the warm orange and copper tones in the fine blades. Provide gritty, well-drained soil and avoid overwatering, especially in winter. The arching foliage looks best cascading over a wall or the rim of a container.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: Set out in sun to light shade in soil that never stays soggy. The pale silver-green blades twist and curl at the tips into a soft, shimmering mound. Rake out loose strands by hand in spring and divide crowded clumps to renew them.

planting.note: Plant in spring in humus-rich woodland soil in shade.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, leafy soil in the shade of trees or a north-facing bed. The broad, puckered green leaves are unusually wide for a sedge and stay handsome all season. Dark flowering stems rise in spring above the strappy evergreen foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a wide range of soils in sun or shade.

directions: Plant this adaptable clumping sedge in almost any soil and either sun or shade. The arching green blades form a graceful fountain useful as a lawn alternative or mass planting. It is drought tolerant once established but greener with occasional water.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Plant divisions at grade in cool, moist soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. The wide, ribbon-like leaves are striped in white and spread slowly by rhizomes into a groundcover. This deciduous sedge dies back in winter and returns fresh each spring.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in moist, well-drained shade or part sun.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and keep it evenly moist through the season. The thin arching blades carry fine gold edges that give the clump a soft glow. Comb out any winter-worn foliage in early spring to refresh the mound.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sun or part shade.

directions: Set out divisions in spring and space them to knit into a low mat over a season or two. The blue-gray blades hold their cool color well and spread modestly by rhizomes. This selection tolerates drought once established and can be sheared in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dry to average shade under trees.

directions: Plant this fine-textured native in dry shade where lawns struggle to grow. The hair-thin green blades cascade into a soft, low mound that needs no mowing. It handles drought and tree-root competition and looks best massed as a groundcover.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained, limey soil in part shade.

directions: Plant this delicate native in well-drained, slightly alkaline soil in dappled shade. The threadlike bright green blades form a soft, low tuft ideal for rock gardens. It spreads slowly and appreciates gritty ground that mimics its rocky native haunts.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average to moist soil in part shade.

directions: Plant this adaptable native in average garden soil in dappled to full shade. The soft green blades form an arching clump that works well massed under trees. It tolerates clay and occasional dampness and stays semi-evergreen in mild winters.

planting.note: Plant in spring in average to moist soil in part shade.

directions: Plant in humus-rich soil in dappled shade at woodland edges. Drooping, long-beaked seed heads hang gracefully from arching stems in late spring. This clumping native tolerates dry shade once established and pairs well with woodland wildflowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist to wet soil in sun or part shade.

directions: Plant this spreading sedge in damp ground at pond margins or in a bog garden. The narrow blue-green blades carry dark, nearly black flower spikes in early summer. It creeps by rhizomes to form colonies and tolerates seasonally flooded soil.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, rich soil in part shade.

directions: Give this large sedge room and consistently moist soil at a pond or stream edge. Long flowering stems arch over and dangle slender catkin-like spikes well above the foliage. It self-sows freely, so remove spent heads if you want to limit seedlings.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Plant this native selection in cool, moist, leafy soil in part to full shade. The wide blue-green blades form a low, spreading clump that lights up shady beds. Keep it evenly watered and comb out any tattered foliage in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained shade.

directions: Set out divisions in cool, moist soil in part shade and keep them from drying out. The narrow dark green blades carry thin silver-white margins on a compact, dense mound. This small evergreen sedge suits edging, rock gardens, and container plantings.

planting.note: Plant after frost in moist, well-drained soil in part shade.

directions: Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in light shade in mild-winter regions. The arching green blades are edged in gold and cascade into a tidy fountain. In cold zones grow it as a container plant and shelter it from hard freezes.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Plant divisions at grade in cool, moist, organic soil in shade to part sun. The broad blades glow banana-yellow with thin green edges and spread slowly into a mat. This deciduous sedge dies down in winter and pushes fresh bright leaves each spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained part shade.

directions: Set the crown at grade in evenly moist, well-drained soil in part shade. The dark green blades carry crisp white margins and arch into a full, mounded clump. Comb out weathered leaves in early spring to keep the variegation looking sharp.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shady soil.

directions: Plant this woodland sedge in damp, shaded ground beside streams or in wet borders. The thin green flower spikes are spaced well apart along arching stems, giving an airy look. It self-sows in moist conditions and tolerates deep shade better than most sedges.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun to keep the coppery-bronze color strong. The very fine, weeping blades form a soft, informal mound that reads warm against greens. Rake out loose old strands by hand rather than cutting the clump back hard.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in humus-rich woodland shade.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, leafy soil in shade for the boldest broad-leaved effect. The wide, puckered green leaves have a seersucker texture and stay evergreen in mild winters. Dark spring flower spikes rise above the strappy foliage in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in bright shade for the strongest color.

directions: Set the crowns level with the soil in rich, well-drained ground that stays lightly moist. This solid chartreuse-gold selection tolerates a bit more sun than the variegated forms but still resents drought. The upright-arching golden blades light up shade gardens from spring until frost.

planting.note: Lift and divide established clumps in spring to propagate.

directions: Plant in cool, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade where crisp white striping shows best. Provide steady moisture and avoid drying winds that brown the leaf tips. The white-and-green blades cascade into a tidy mound that flushes pink in cold autumn weather.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; fall color intensifies with cool nights.

directions: Set divisions into rich, moisture-retentive soil in light shade to partial sun. Keep the root zone cool and evenly watered through summer. The name means red wind, and the green summer foliage turns fiery red and orange as the season closes.

planting.note: Divide in spring; this compact form is ideal for containers.

directions: Plant this compact selection in moist, fertile soil in partial shade, spacing plants close for a full look. Keep it watered and cool through the growing season. The fine green blades color brilliantly to orange and red in autumn on a smaller, tidy mound.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions in bright shade for good fall tints.

directions: Site in cool, organic soil in partial shade and keep it reliably moist. Refresh mulch each spring to conserve moisture and feed the crown. The gold-green summer foliage transitions to warm coppery reds as nights turn cold.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring; protect the fine white striping from sun.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist soil in shade to partial shade. Shelter the finely white-flecked leaves from strong afternoon sun and drying wind. The frosty, snow-dusted appearance brightens dark corners and picks up a pink cast in cool fall weather.

common_name: Stripe It Rich Japanese Forest Grass

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in bright shade for crisp gold and white.

directions: Set into fertile, moisture-retentive soil in partial shade and keep it evenly watered. This sport of All Gold shows narrow white margins on gold blades that read brightest with morning light. The cascading foliage forms a luminous mound that colors softly in autumn.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions; fall red flush is especially strong.

directions: Plant in rich, moist soil in bright shade where gold foliage will not scorch. Water regularly and mulch to keep the crown cool. The bright gold blades develop dramatic red and burgundy tones as the growing season winds down.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring in bright, sheltered shade.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in partial shade with morning sun. Keep the soil consistently damp and top-dress with compost each spring. The green-and-gold variegated blades arch into a vigorous mound that warms to pink and red in cool weather.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the fibrous crown level with the soil in a spot with steady moisture and good drainage. Comb out old and browned blades in late winter to freshen the clump. The arching leaves carry a broad creamy-yellow center stripe and stay handsome nearly year-round in mild climates.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist soil or containers.

directions: Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in partial shade where its color pops against darker foliage. Keep it watered and tidy up winter-worn leaves in spring. The cascading blades emerge lime green and mature to a solid glowing gold.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; grow as an annual accent in cold zones.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun to partial shade and avoid waterlogging over winter. The wiry, weeping bronze-brown blades add fine texture to beds and mixed containers. Trim out any dead strands in spring to keep the fountain shape neat.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; use as a container accent in cold regions.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in sun to light shade, spacing to show off the weeping habit. The very fine, pale silvery-green blades curl at the tips into a soft mound. Rake out dead foliage in early spring and divide crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich woodland soil.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, evenly moist soil in shade, mimicking its native woodland floor. The broad, puckered strap-like leaves give this native its seersucker name and stay semi-evergreen. Slender dark flower spikes rise in spring above the low clumps.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, shaded ground.

directions: Set into moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade and keep it from drying out. The stiff, arching dark green blades carry crisp white margins for a bright evergreen mound. Comb or shear away tattered leaves at winter's end to refresh the clump.
